speaker: interviewer
text: Freddy Diament. Roll seven. with Freddy Diament.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know what I forgot to tell you?

speaker: interviewer
text: Freddy, there-- there is a story you've forgotten to tell me. It's a good time to tell me now.

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of the Hungarian Jews were sent to our camp, to Auschwitz III Buna. And among them was also a little boy, a yeshiva bocher. His name was Elie Wiesel. And interesting enough, also the other famous Holocaust writer, Primo Levi, was also in-- in Auschwitz. I knew him also. Nobody knew at that time what they will be in the future. There was a old time inmate in the-- they call this [GERMAN]. The-- the-- the-- the-- the office where all the work commanders were designated and were put together. And it was decided how many people will work in certain commanders when they work, I told you, in this giant factory. And his name was [NON-ENGLISH]. This man, before the war, was a publisher of religious books, like Tanakh, Chumash, Gemara. Wonderful guy. And he was a religious Jew. He was looking out for young kids who were religious. And he discovered Elie, because Elie was a yeshiva bocher. And I-- he also-- for me, he was like a spiritual father. He also talked to me many times because he was afraid that I might be being brainwashed by the communists. Taught me about Jewish heritage, about Jewish life, and-- and Jewish tradition. And of course, when I told him that my father was an orthodox Jew, my father was a [YIDDISH]. He found interest. And this friendship lasted for many, many years. Anyway-- so then after the war when I came to United States, I met Elie, I think in Washington. I went to Washington in connection with the Holocaust Museum.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. The other way around. Elie came to visit Los Angeles and spoke here. And I approached him. I said, do you remember me from-- from-- from Auschwitz? He said not really, but I-- I-- I know-- he-- he wrote also in his book about the hanging of my brother. He was-- he was there. He was also in the-- in the crowd. And he knew that was my brother. That he knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he invited me to get actively involved in a museum in Washington. And he appointed me-- there was a small board of advisers to the chairman. He was a chairman, a founding chairman. And he appoint me as adviser to a chairman. And anytime they had a meeting there, the council, he invited me and I went there. Then Elie resigned, if you remember. And-- and I especially invite him here and had a big talk with him. And we sit in a night club with the board. He made up his mind, no. Anyway, when--

speaker: interviewer
text: No what?

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't want to go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I see.

speaker: interviewer
text: You tried to--

speaker: interviewee
text: --begged him to go back. Elie, how can you-- this is your lifetime accomplishment. I said, without you there would be no museum. He said--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you know why he didn't want to go back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I know. He would-- he couldn't get along with-- with the American builders there. He-- he-- he said they are too-- too trivial, he said. They really-- it is not only important, he said, to build a museum. The content of the museum is the most important thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are we going to present to the world, and what is going to be with this museum in 150, in 200 years? Will the Jewish content, will the centrality of the Holocaust, be the theme of the museum? Or will it become-- I see-- I-- I know what he said. Like-- like the Shoah Museum of the-- of-- of the [INAUDIBLE]. That-- that is all over the place.

speaker: interviewee
text: He wanted to build a museum that focused and strictly is concerned with the Shoah, with the tragedy that befell the Jewish people all over Europe. That was his concern. Anyway, he recommended me when they build-- and-- started to really creating the structure, to-- to put together a plan what the museum will show. What will be shown, how much will be shown, in what sequence, and how it will be shown, too strong, too soft, in middle. And I was appointed to be a member of the content committee. It was probably the most important committee. That decided, really, what the museum will be. It was an international committee of 15 people, and I must have been 50 times in Washington six-- five, six years prior to the museum was finished. And I think that was very rewarding to me. And I think I get some good ideas there, and I opened up my mouth and I spoke. And-- and they listened to me. And the chairman of this committee was Ben Meed and Michael Berenbaum. You heard about Michael Berenbaum. And they were very much concerned there will be a strong input by survivors, because if they do it too antiseptic, too academic, it will lose its appeal, and will lose its soul, so to speak. And-- and-- Sam Katz also. I arranged that Sam Katz also would be appointed, because Sam Katz was not involved in this. There were altogether, I think, eight survivors, and the rest were academicians. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we had-- we had some-- some strong arguments there. Because as I said, we insisted that the individual should be the focus of-- of this museum so that people can identify with it. And then-- then-- then it has an impact. If it is all reduced to individual suffering, it might have an impact. If you talk about 6 million, it becomes abstract and antiseptic, and it-- it loses its effect. And-- and they-- they accepted that, that promise-- that-- that premise.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in all honesty, it is by far better than I ever expected. That museum is-- is incredible. You have been there, no? It's unbelievable. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that was also part of the motivation that kept me involved in-- in-- in-- in remembrance of the Holocaust, obviously.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and I'm-- I'm in contact with Elie. Every time he's here, we get together, so-- I talk to him once in a while. He talks to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, Freddy, you didn't mention Martyrs Memorial in the Museum of the Holocaust where you were also--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --on the content committee.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had a great deal of input into its function.

speaker: interviewee
text: How do you know that? You were also in the committee, no? That's right. [LAUGHS] Yeah. No. I-- I got involved in Martyrs Memorial very early on. As a matter of fact, I remember when there was only a vision, a dream. When you were still in-- on Vermont, we talked about the Martyrs memorial. And we were concerned at that time to get a place in a prominent place in-- in a-- in a civic-- in a civic area downtown Los Angeles. We wanted to build a museum that will be accessible also by non-Jews. But apparently, that-- that never came to-- to-- to-- to be implemented in-- to-- never became a reality, let's put it this way. And then when the Jewish committee moved here, then the chances were-- were-- were by far better that-- that they have a Martyrs Memorial. I will be honest with you, the Martyrs Memorial of the Holocaust can be very, very effective under one condition, that it has-- if it has adequate funding. And it doesn't. I give you an example. There was-- six or seven years ago, we had almost every day a bus full of children coming. At that time, apparently the school-- Unified School District had enough money to afford a bus. In the last two, three years, they have no budget, and we have-- our budget is-- is peanuts, so to speak. And-- and the museum itself is not utilized properly because we need money to bring people in. On the other hand, we have our very talented director that does occasionally quite a bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: But my point is that you are very much involved--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in all the issues that-- of Holocaust remembrance.

speaker: interviewee
text: I also shared four Yom HaShoah. The-- the big-- and-- and I was the main speaker four times at Yom HaShoah Commemoration, in--

speaker: interviewer
text: So has your family shared in your interest in-- of the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife. The kids, for some reason, I have a problem with them. They feel that it's too painful. And every time I-- I talk about-- especially my oldest daughter. She's very, very sensitive. She-- she really suffers from this. And-- and-- and-- and-- and every-- any time that she watches something, for example, on television about the Holocaust, she-- she has nightmares. And-- and-- and she tells me, Dad, it's enough that you suffered. I know enough about it I don't want to go over the details all the time. I would like them to be active, but they are not.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your children doing these days?

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest son and my youngest son, they're both in business. They have the two wrecking yards the-- for foreign cars. And-- and-- and they're doing very well. My daughter was for four years a teacher, Elana. And now she had some argument there, and the school is-- well, a bit [INAUDIBLE]. They are consolidating. There are not enough children. So she-- she was for seven years with me in business. And she has a tremendous background in-- in the garment industry. And now she's an assistant manager for Gap. And soon she'll be a manager too, there, I know. She-- she's very good. And my oldest daughter--

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Amalia. She doesn't do it. She-- she has problems, and she has no-- she claims that because we talk too much about the Holocaust. It frightened her. She is-- she-- she has a hard time with coping.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Coping with life.

speaker: interviewer
text: --two boys' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest one is Jeff, and the younger one is Stevie.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I wanted now to ask you, Freddy, whether you have something that you want to say to the future generations. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Your own great grandchildren, or future generation of people altogether.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- what I would like to say is, first of all, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] if we really want to remember the 6 million, then it's important that the Jewish civilization would survive and would continue to grow and to flourish. And-- and they should be part of it. I also feel the state of Israel is central. The state of Israel is central to Jewish survival. And it's the obligation of every Jew to support the state of Israel whenever he can. That's important. And also, Jews, the living Diaspora, should not lose the contact with Israel. Should visit there. Should get inspiration from there. And if they feel they can live in Israeli society, that they make aliyah. I myself would not hesitate to go back to Israel. But Ilse, she's so close with grandchildren, she wouldn't want to-- to leave the grandchildren. I can understand that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think survivors have an obligation to make the world more sensitive to the suffering of others. That's important. And if you can-- if you can transmit this idea to the next generation and the next generation, we have accomplished a lot. Because the world-- oh, let's put it this way. Elie Wiesel said it very, very powerful. He said, there is almost an infinite capacity for evil in man, but there's also an infinite capacity for good in man. And we have to be-- the survivors and the children, grand of survivors, have to be on the forefront. On the forefront of those people that will foster the good in man and make the world more sensitive to the suffering of others. And also that we have an obligation to relieve the suffering of others. You know, part of the Jewish tradition is Tikkun Olam, that anybody that lives in this world has an obligation to make this a better world than it was when he entered this world. And-- and although we live now in-- in a-- in a century of egotism and-- and-- and self-gratification and-- and-- and-- and primarily think of themselves, people should have a social conscience and think of others too. Because after all, we are our brother's keeper. And-- and-- and I would also like that the Jewish people will remember one of the most important principles why Judaism did survive is [SPEAKING HEBREW]. That we are responsible for each other, for Jews that are in-- in distress, the Jews that live under-- under-- under-- call it under tyranny, what have you. In other words, we don't live only for ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Freddy, I want to thank you very much for doing this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. It's my pleasure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Freddy, please introduce your wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Ilse. We are married [YIDDISH] 47 years Ilse is a survivor herself. As a matter of fact, she's a child survivor. And many years ago, she did a tape with UCLA.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it's a little bit painful for her. She's a little bit more sensitive than I am, apparently. We have four children and four grandchildren. Isn't that interesting? Yeah. And I think she's the best grandmother in town. Are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ilse, where are you from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a little-- little village. We were only one Jewish family.

speaker: interviewee
text: What name? The name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Krefeld. ?] It's not on a map.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was not a Jewish school, because we were the only family. So they sent my-- us to Stettin. It's a big city.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: To school.

speaker: interviewee
text: To school.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother and my sister and me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of your family survive? And

speaker: interviewee
text: I had two sisters in Israel before the war. Now that's--

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. And they're the ones-- and you met when you were 15 and 1/2, I understand?

speaker: interviewee
text: To whom?

speaker: interviewer
text: Freddy. How old were you when you met him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Almost--

speaker: interviewee
text: Almost 16.

speaker: interviewer
text: Almost 16. Whatever. Anyhow, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] tell me about your grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody has to tell their own story, the wonderful people, children. Well-- my oldest son has lots and lots of feelings, and he doesn't want to hurt us with anything. He went with us to opening to museum in Washington, DC. And he-- he asked me all kinds of questions.

speaker: interviewee
text: The grandchildren also went with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said Ricky.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. Jeff, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Jeff, too. My son. My grandson.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went with your son, Jeff--

speaker: interviewee
text: Son. My daughter, Elana.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my grandson, the son of Jeff.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ricky.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ricky. I see. How old-- when was Ricky born, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's 14 now.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. OK. And you have other grandchildren. What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Justin, Marissa, and Austin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have a big smile when you say that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I love them very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father, Simon Diament. This picture was taken around 1917 during the First World War in Poland. He was a soldier in the Austrian Army. This is my mother. This picture was taken around 19-- I would say 19-- before the World War. 1912, 1913. She was then about 16 years old, and she had a fashion store in Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Amalia Weiss.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this store?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Vienna. Exactly where, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture is taken around, I would say, 1928 or 1929. There are the three boys of the Diaments. The oldest one on the left side, his name is Saul Diament. In the mid is Leo Diament. He is the one that unfortunately was hanged in Auschwitz. And on the right side, that's me. Freddy Diament.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken either 1928 or 1929 in Germany. In Gelsenkirchen, Germany. I want to correct myself. On the picture, in the middle that's me, Freddy Diament. On the right side, that is my brother, Leo Diament. And this is my brother Leo. This picture was taken in 1936. He was then 15 years old, and a student at the [GERMAN] Gymnasium in Gelsenkirchen, Germany. Leo was-- is my brother that unfortunately did not make it. He was together with me five and a half years, and then in October 1944 was hanged by the Nazis for conspiracy to arrange a mass outbreak from Auschwitz. This is a picture that was taken in 1945. Right after the war. I would say it could have been either June or July in Kibbutz Buchenwald. Kibbutz Buchenwald was a preparation farm for people that had the intention to go to Palestine. The-- the picture-- the-- the-- my-- my person is marked with an X, and my wife, Ilse, on the left side on the bottom is also marked with an X.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your wife to be?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. At that time, she was my girlfriend. Yeah. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not even a girlfriend yet. She ignored me. Anyway, you can see there were all young people. And they all came from-- from all kinds of camps from-- from all over Germany, mostly from Buchenwald. And we stick together even after we arrived in then Palestine under the British mandate. This is a picture on-- out the sea to get illegally to then Palestine. This is a picture of the-- of the ship. It was a freighter. It was transformed into passenger ship. The conditions were very, very difficult. We were mostly sleeping underneath, and the-- the top of the ship was covered with planes so to fool the British airplanes that were constantly patrolling the Mediterranean to prevent Jewish people from coming illegally to then Palestine. As a matter of fact, we were caught about 50 kilometers before Haifa by a British destroyer that by force boarded our ship and took us all as prisoners to the harbor, where we were then interned into Atlit detention camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of myself, Freddy Diament, in Kibbutz Netzer Sereni. At the time, I was the [HEBREW]. In-- in-- in English translation, I was the general manager of the kibbutz. And I was running around most of the time on the horse, which I liked very much. By the way, this is a full-bred Arabian horse that was conquered by [INAUDIBLE] during the war. And then they kept those horses for-- for months and months and months in our kibbutz. So I took the opportunity and was riding around whenever I could. This is a picture of our two children who were born in Israel. On the left side is Amalia, our daughter Amalia. On the right side is our older son. At that time, they called him Yehuda, and now he's called Jeff. I would say it was either 1958 or 1959 in Haifa. With dimples. This is a picture of my youngest son, Stevie, and his son, our grandson, Austin. This picture was taken in 1995. Our grandson Austin is then about two years old. That is in his new house, new home, in the kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: In which city?

speaker: interviewee
text: He lives in also Studio City.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: The same city as we do. This is a picture that was taken at the wedding of my youngest daughter, Elana, on the left side, and her husband Gary Cooperman. And then next to it is my wife, Ilse. She looks very, very happy, as you can see, that she got rid of her daughter. [LAUGHS]. And next to her, that's me, myself, Fred Diament. The picture was taken at the wedding at the Beth Shalom Temple in Encino in 1995. This is a picture, a family picture, which was taken at the wedding of my daughter Elana in June of 1995 at Temple Beth Shalom in Encino. From left to right, on the extreme left is my son Steven and his wife, Harriet. Next to Harriet is my daughter Amalia, my older daughter. Then it's myself, Freddy Diament. Then it's my daughter, the bride, Elana Diament. And next to it is her husband, of course, Gary Cooperman. And then it's my wife, Ilse. And then standing is Shelley Diament, my oldest son-- son's wife. Then it's my oldest son, Jeff. Then to the right of my oldest son is Ricky, my oldest son. At that time, he was 13 years old. Before him is his brother, Justin, who at that time was about seven years old. And then before him is his sister Marissa, our grandchild. She at that time was about five years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so Austin is missing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
